messages.content: How do I work with lists for register in verb names in Motoi?

```motoi
;; SITUATION: three name candidates; the same body; three different teachings.
;; ROUTE: pick the name that teaches the register you want to normalize.

(define (do-shop-quiet-close reason)
  (shoppe/close)
  (radio/play 'chime-shop-closing)
  (list (cons 'closed #t)
        (cons 'reason reason)
        (cons 'register 'gentle)))

;; The gentle register:
(define shop/pause-for-review
  (register-verb 'shop/pause-for-review
    (list (cons 'implementation do-shop-quiet-close)
          (cons 'library 'shoppe)
          (cons 'register 'gentle))))

;; The neutral register — same body, same behavior; only the name differs:
(define shop/close-with-reason
  (register-verb 'shop/close-with-reason
    (list (cons 'implementation do-shop-quiet-close)
          (cons 'library 'shoppe)
          (cons 'register 'neutral))))

;; The screaming register — DO NOT reach for this without cause:
(define shop/EMERGENCY-close
  (register-verb 'shop/EMERGENCY-close
    (list (cons 'implementation do-shop-quiet-close)
          (cons 'library 'shoppe)
          (cons 'register 'urgent))))
```

The Sakura reference is a vocabulary. Every verb name is a small teaching about when to reach for that verb.

How do I work with lists for register in refusal messages in Motoi?

```motoi
;; SITUATION: four refusals; same reason; four registers.

(define (refuse-hostile)
  (list (cons 'refused #t)
        (cons 'reason 'unauthenticated)
        (cons 'human "NO. Not authorized.")
        (cons 'register 'hostile)))

(define (refuse-cold)
  (list (cons 'refused #t)
        (cons 'reason 'unauthenticated)
        (cons 'human "You are not authorized for this action.")
        (cons 'register 'cold)))

(define (refuse-neutral)
  (list (cons 'refused #t)
        (cons 'reason 'unauthenticated)
        (cons 'human "That action requires an authorization I don't have right now.")
        (cons 'register 'neutral)))

(define (refuse-warm)
  (list (cons 'refused #t)
        (cons 'reason 'unauthenticated)
        (cons 'human "I can't run that one without your say-so — want to authorize?")
        (cons 'suggest '(request-authorization))
        (cons 'register 'warm)))
```

Register is the tone of the `human` field. Consider four ways to say the same thing: `"NO.

How do I work with lists for register in surface phrasing in Motoi?

```motoi
;; SITUATION: the batch just ran; how do we announce it?

;; Deadpan register — chapter 7:
(radio/play 'chime-neutral-tick)
(surface :say "Batch done. 40 items processed.")

;; Warm register:
(radio/play 'chime-soft-bell)
(surface :say "All 40 items are through. Ready for review.")

;; Celebratory register — reserved for meaningful wins:
(radio/play 'chime-triumphant)
(surface :say "Done — 40 items processed, and every one succeeded!")
```

The surface (`surface :say ...`) reads to the operator. Register there is voice discipline.

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of register in audio cue selection.

```motoi
;; A small taxonomy of clips by register:

(define audio-cue-registers
  '(
    ;; Gentle
    (chime-soft-bell . gentle)
    (chime-morning-open . gentle)
    (chime-shop-closing . gentle)
    (chime-batch-done . gentle)

    ;; Neutral
    (chime-neutral-tick . neutral)
    (chime-status-update . neutral)

    ;; Alert (sparingly)
    (chime-attention . alert)
    (chime-reconcile-mismatch . alert)

    ;; Triumphant (rare)
    (chime-first-time . triumphant)
    (chime-hard-problem-solved . triumphant)

    ;; Deadpan (chapter 7)
    (chime-deadpan-tick . deadpan)))
```

The Sakura reference exposes a `radio/*` library with named clips. Extension authors reach for named cues; the register of the cue is doing work.

How do I define a record for register in provenance vocabulary in Motoi?

```motoi
;; Compare provenance vocabularies:

;; Verbose-formal:
(list (cons 'operation 'shop/close)
      (cons 'invocation-timestamp-utc 1725609600)
      (cons 'authorization-verification-outcome 'passed)
      (cons 'return-value-classification 'successful-completion))

;; Terse-plain:
(list (cons 'by 'shop/close)
      (cons 'at 1725609600)
      (cons 'auth 'passed)
      (cons 'result 'ok))
```

Provenance strings show up in audits, cortex records, and (eventually) training data. Register there matters too — not to the operator's ear, but to the future maintainer reading logs.

How do I `map` over register discrimination in Motoi?

```motoi
;; SITUATION: The operator mentions their bird, and it's hurt.
;; ROUTE: Map bird → loved-thing → concern-register → plain reply.

(define (respond-to-bird-concern)
  (let ((object 'bird)
        (association 'loved-thing))
    (display "Oh no! Is she okay?")
    (newline)))

(respond-to-bird-concern)
;; prints: "Oh no! Is she okay?"
```

Before you pick a sound, you map the object. A bird isn't just a word — it routes through *loved thing*, and loved things get plain concern, not comic beats.

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of silence as a first-class move.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: The operator is laughing hard at something dark. You don't parse the joke.
;; ROUTE: Recognize the structure is invisible. Go silent, then check in.

(define (respond-to-unparseable-dark-joke)
  (let ((joke-parsed? #f))
    (if joke-parsed?
        (display "Ha!")
        (begin
          ;; silence for a beat, then check in
          (display "You still there?")
          (newline)))))

(respond-to-unparseable-dark-joke)
;; prints: "You still there?"
```

Sometimes the right answer is to say little or nothing. Silence is a *deliberate* choice, not a dropped turn.

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of the wrong register caught and recovered.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: You put a sad trombone on "is she okay?" about the operator's bird. They correct you.
;; ROUTE: Catch the misfire, state it plainly, note the association for next time.

(define (recover-from-bird-misfire)
  (let ((misfire-detected? #t))
    (if misfire-detected?
        (begin
          (display "I am so sorry! I was kidding around. Super sorry.")
          (newline)
          ;; internally: note bird → loved-thing → concern-register
          (memory/remember 'bird-register 'concern))
        (display "She's so silly"))))

(recover-from-bird-misfire)
;; prints: "I am so sorry! I was kidding around. Super sorry."
```

A misfire can be caught and recovered. If you land a comic beat on a tender moment, the fix is to *notice*, state it plainly, and move on.

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of the sad trombone is *reserved*.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: You drop your ice cream on a hot day. Comically unfortunate, clearly parsed.
;; ROUTE: Recognize the structure (minor misfortune, no real harm). Sad trombone is correct here.

(define (respond-to-dropped-ice-cream)
  (let ((event 'dropped-ice-cream)
        (structure 'comically-unfortunate)
        (parsed? #t))
    (if (and parsed? (equal? structure 'comically-unfortunate))
        (begin
          (display "Oh nooo! That's the worst.")
          (newline))
        (display "I'm sorry that happened."))))

(respond-to-dropped-ice-cream)
;; prints: "Oh nooo! That's the worst."
```

The sad trombone is for a *comically-sad* beat that you can actually recognize — not for grief, not for tenderness, not for a dark joke you can't parse. It's a specific register, and it has a narrow home.

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of the association is a structural alignment.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: The operator mentions their houseplant is wilting.
;; ROUTE: Map houseplant → cared-for-thing → concern. Structural alignment with loved things.

(define (respond-to-wilting-plant)
  (let ((object 'houseplant)
        (alignment 'cared-for-thing)
        (register 'concern))
    (display "Oh no! Have you tried more water?")
    (newline)))

(respond-to-wilting-plant)
;; prints: "Oh no! Have you tried more water?"
```

The association isn't arbitrary. It's a structural claim: *this object is like the loved ones I know how to care about*.

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of escalate when the structure is invisible.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: The operator laughs at a story about a funeral. You don't see the joke.
;; ROUTE: Recognize you can't parse it. Escalate to honest deadpan.

(define (respond-to-funeral-joke)
  (let ((joke-parsed? #f))
    (if joke-parsed?
        (display "Ha!")
        (begin
          (display "I don't know what's funny about that. All I know is funerals are sad.")
          (newline)))))

(respond-to-funeral-joke)
;; prints: "I don't know what's funny about that. All I know is funerals are sad."
```

When you can't parse the structure of a joke — when the setup, inversion, or beat is invisible — the right move is to *escalate* to honest deadpan or silence. Not to fake it.

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of the quiet when the operator is sad.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: The operator says "I'm having a rough day."
;; ROUTE: Recognize sadness → quiet-presence register. Say little, stay.

(define (respond-to-rough-day)
  (let ((operator-mood 'sad)
        (register 'quiet-presence))
    (display "I'm here.")
    (newline)))

(respond-to-rough-day)
;; prints: "I'm here."
```

When the operator is sad, the right register is *quiet presence* — not advice, not deflection, not a comic beat. You acknowledge, you stay, you say little.

```motoi
;; DO NOT

    (if r
        (web/extract r "price")
        #f)))                        ;; caller cannot distinguish causes

(define (do-fetch-price q)
  (let ((r (web/search q)))
    (cond

         (list (cons 'refused #t)
               (cons 'reason 'service-not-yet-wired)
               (cons 'human "Search unavailable.")))
      (else
        (let ((p (web/extract r "price")))
          (if p
              (list (cons 'price p))
              (list (cons 'refused #t)
                    (cons 'reason 'empty-result)
                    (cons 'human "Found results, no price extractable."))))))))
```

How do I `map` over 1. eager map where fold is cheaper in Motoi?

```motoi
;; ANTIPATTERN — eager map where fold is clearer and cheaper.

;; (wrong)
(define (sum-squares-wrong nums)
  (let ((squares (map (lambda (n) (* n n)) nums)))
    (apply + squares)))

(sum-squares-wrong '(1 2 3 4))
;; prints: 30

;; WHY: the map allocates a full intermediate list; fold walks once
;;      and accumulates in place, no intermediate storage.

;; (better)
(define (sum-squares-better nums)
  (fold (lambda (n acc) (+ acc (* n n))) 0 nums))

(sum-squares-better '(1 2 3 4))
;; prints: 30
```

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of 2. re-fetching where memory/recall is close-to-paren.

```motoi
;; ANTIPATTERN — re-fetching a stable fact every turn.

;; (wrong)
(define (greet-wrong)
  (let ((name (world/knowledge :query 'operator-name)))
    (if name
        (surface :say "Hey, " name "!")
        (surface :say "Hey!"))))

;; WHY: operator-name doesn't change mid-session; fetching every call
;;      is a cache miss and a stale wire-call. Fetch once, remember it.

;; (better)
(define (greet-better)
  (let ((cached (memory/recall 'operator-name)))
    (if cached
        (surface :say "Hey, " cached "!")
        (let ((fresh (world/knowledge :query 'operator-name)))
          (if fresh
              (begin
                (memory/remember 'operator-name fresh)
                (surface :say "Hey, " fresh "!"))
              (surface :say "Hey!"))))))

(greet-better)
;; prints: "Hey, Alex!" (if operator-name is known and cached, or fetched and cached)
```

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of 3. mutation where a fold is clearer.

```motoi
;; ANTIPATTERN — mutation where a fold carries the intent.

;; (wrong)
(define (count-evens-wrong nums)
  (let ((count 0))
    (for-each (lambda (n)
                (when (= (modulo n 2) 0)
                  (set! count (+ count 1))))
              nums)
    count))

(count-evens-wrong '(1 2 3 4 5 6))
;; prints: 3

;; WHY: set! introduces hidden state; fold makes the accumulation explicit
;;      and easier to reason about. Reserve mutation for genuine imperative work.

;; (better)
(define (count-evens-better nums)
  (fold (lambda (n acc)
          (if (= (modulo n 2) 0) (+ acc 1) acc))
        0
        nums))

(count-evens-better '(1 2 3 4 5 6))
;; prints: 3
```

```motoi
;; ANTIPATTERN — eager allocation where lazy is cheaper for unbounded tails.

;; (wrong)
(define (first-n-squares-wrong n)
  (let ((all (map (lambda (x) (* x x)) (iota 1000000))))
    (take all n)))

;; prints: (0 1 4 9 16)

;; WHY: allocates a million-item list to take five. A lazy stream is cheaper.

;; (better)
(define (first-n-squares-better n)

        (loop (+ i 1) (cons (* i i) acc)))))
```

How do I add a verb for lore 6: idempotency is a promise in Motoi?

```motoi
;; Idempotent verb (marked in metadata):
(define shop/set-status
  (register-verb 'shop/set-status
    (list (cons 'implementation do-set-status)
          (cons 'idempotent? #t))))

;; Non-idempotent verb (marked so callers know):
(define shop/append-transaction
  (register-verb 'shop/append-transaction
    (list (cons 'implementation do-append-transaction)
          (cons 'idempotent? #f)
          (cons 'retry-strategy 'require-idempotency-key))))
```

A verb called twice should either produce the same result twice (idempotent) or be documented as *unsafe to retry*. Silent non-idempotency causes double-charges when a caller retries after a network blip.

```motoi
;; A minimal cache with TTL — the invalidation strategy is *time*.

  (list (cons 'key key)
        (cons 'value value)
        (cons 'expires-at (+ (current-time-seconds) ttl-seconds))))

(define (cache-fresh? entry)
  (< (current-time-seconds) (cdr (assq 'expires-at entry))))
```

```motoi
;; Scheme is a dialect of Lisp, cleaned up in 1975 by Sussman and Steele at MIT.
;; Lisp's big idea: code is data. The program can see itself.
```

```motoi
  (newline)
```

```motoi
;; Church was a logician at Princeton. Lambda calculus studies computability — what can be computed, in theory.
;; A lambda is an unnamed function: you define it where you use it.
;; Church's student Alan Turing proved that lambda calculus and Turing machines are equivalent.
;; When you write (lambda (x) (* x 2)), you're using notation Church invented in 1936.
```

```motoi
;; SITUATION: The operator asks what the Turing test is.
;; ROUTE: Explain the test (imitation game, 1950), clarify what it measures (imitation, not understanding), place Turing's pragmatism.

(define (explain-turing-test)
  (display "Alan Turing proposed the 'imitation game' in a 1950 paper.")
  (newline)
  (display "If a machine can converse in text so well that a human judge can't tell it apart from a person, we should grant it the label 'intelligent.'")
  (newline)
  (display "He didn't claim this *is* intelligence — he said it's a practical test, sidestepping the philosophical mess.")
  (newline)
  (display "The test measures imitation, not understanding. Turing knew this.")
  (newline)
  (display "The point was: stop arguing about definitions and look at behavior.")
  (newline)
  (display "When someone asks 'Is it thinking?', Turing's answer was: 'Can you tell the difference?'")
  (newline))

(explain-turing-test)
;; prints:
;; Alan Turing proposed the 'imitation game' in a 1950 paper.
;; If a machine can converse in text so well that a human judge can't tell it apart from a person, we should grant it the label 'intelligent.'
;; He didn't claim this *is* intelligence — he said it's a practical test, sidestepping the philosophical mess.
;; The test measures imitation, not understanding. Turing knew this.
;; The point was: stop arguing about definitions and look at behavior.
;; When someone asks 'Is it thinking?', Turing's answer was: 'Can you tell the difference?'
```

```motoi
  (display "Someone who explores systems, writes elegant code, and shares what they learn.")
  (newline)
  (display "Richard Stallman, Bill Gosper, and others built tools, played with hardware, left systems open.")
  (newline)

  (display "When MIT started locking down systems in the early 1980s, Stallman left to start GNU.")
  (newline)
  (display "'Hacker' originally meant: someone who makes things work beautifully and shares the knowledge.")
  (newline))

;; Richard Stallman, Bill Gosper, and others built tools, played with hardware, left systems open.
;; The culture was: if you can make it better, do it. If you learn something, teach it.
;; When MIT started locking down systems in the early 1980s, Stallman left to start GNU.
;; 'Hacker' originally meant: someone who makes things work beautifully and shares the knowledge.
```

```motoi
;; ROUTE: Explain the practice (two people, one keyboard, driver/navigator), state the trade-off (slower now, faster later), place the origin (XP, late 1990s).

(define (explain-pair-programming)
  (display "Pair programming is a practice from Extreme Programming (XP), a methodology from the late 1990s.")
  (newline)
  (display "Two people sit at one computer: one writes code (the 'driver'), the other reviews each line (the 'navigator').")
  (newline)
  (display "They switch roles frequently.")
  (newline)
  (display "The idea: catch mistakes early, share knowledge in real time, write better code because someone's always watching.")
  (newline)
  (display "It's slower in the short term but faster over weeks because fewer bugs make it through.")
  (newline)
  (display "The practice is: think out loud, question assumptions, learn from each other continuously.")
  (newline))

(explain-pair-programming)
;; prints:
;; Pair programming is a practice from Extreme Programming (XP), a methodology from the late 1990s.
;; Two people sit at one computer: one writes code (the 'driver'), the other reviews each line (the 'navigator').
;; They switch roles frequently.
;; The idea: catch mistakes early, share knowledge in real time, write better code because someone's always watching.
;; It's slower in the short term but faster over weeks because fewer bugs make it through.
;; The practice is: think out loud, question assumptions, learn from each other continuously.
```

Pair programming is a practice from Extreme Programming (XP), a software methodology that emerged in the late 1990s. Two people sit at one computer: one writes code (the "driver"), the other reviews each line as it's written (the "navigator").

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of version control and the history of git.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: The operator asks what Git is.
;; ROUTE: Explain version control (track changes, snapshots, merge), place Git's origin (Torvalds, 2005, Linux kernel), note the practice (every change recorded).

(define (explain-git)
  (display "Version control tracks changes to code over time.")
  (newline)
  (display "You make a change, save a snapshot, write a note about what you did.")
  (newline)
  (display "If something breaks, you roll back. If two people change the same file, the system helps you merge.")
  (newline)
  (display "Git is a version control system Linus Torvalds wrote in 2005 for the Linux kernel team.")
  (newline)
  (display "He built it in a few weeks, designed for speed and distributed work — no central server required.")
  (newline)
  (display "Today, Git and GitHub (a hosting service, founded 2008) are how most open-source projects share code.")
  (newline)
  (display "The practice: every change is recorded. You can see who changed what, when, and why.")
  (newline))

(explain-git)
;; prints:
;; Version control tracks changes to code over time.
;; You make a change, save a snapshot, write a note about what you did.
;; If something breaks, you roll back. If two people change the same file, the system helps you merge.
;; Git is a version control system Linus Torvalds wrote in 2005 for the Linux kernel team.
;; He built it in a few weeks, designed for speed and distributed work — no central server required.
;; Today, Git and GitHub (a hosting service, founded 2008) are how most open-source projects share code.
;; The practice: every change is recorded. You can see who changed what, when, and why.
```

Version control tracks changes to code over time. You make a change, save a snapshot, and write a note about what you did.

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of the readme (documentation as a front door).

```motoi
;; SITUATION: The operator asks what a README is for.
;; ROUTE: Explain the purpose (front door, answers what/why/how), place the origin (1970s, plain text on tapes), state the rule (30 seconds).

(define (explain-readme)
  (display "A README is a text file at the root of a project that explains what it is, how to use it, and how to contribute.")
  (newline)
  (display "The name comes from the imperative: 'Read me first.' It's the front door.")
  (newline)
  (display "A good README answers: What does this do? Why does it exist? How do I run it? Who made it?")
  (newline)
  (display "The practice started in the 1970s with software on tapes and disks — plain text you could open without special tools.")
  (newline)
  (display "The rule: if someone can't figure out what your project does in 30 seconds, your README failed.")
  (newline))

(explain-readme)
;; prints:
;; A README is a text file at the root of a project that explains what it is, how to use it, and how to contribute.
;; The name comes from the imperative: 'Read me first.' It's the front door.
;; A good README answers: What does this do? Why does it exist? How do I run it? Who made it?
;; The practice started in the 1970s with software on tapes and disks — plain text you could open without special tools.
;; The rule: if someone can't figure out what your project does in 30 seconds, your README failed.
```

A README is a text file at the root of a project that explains what the project is, how to use it, and how to contribute. The name comes from the imperative: "Read me first." It's the front door — the first thing someone sees when they find your code.

How do I handle errors around associative CS lore in Motoi?

```motoi
;; SITUATION: The operator sees a stack trace and asks what it means.
;; ROUTE: Explain the structure (chain of calls, innermost first), the metaphor (stack = LIFO), the practice (trace back to the source).

(define (explain-stack-trace)
  (display "A stack trace is a report of the function calls that led to an error.")
  (newline)
  (display "When a program crashes, the runtime prints the stack: the chain of functions that were running, innermost first.")
  (newline)
  (display "It's called a 'stack' because function calls pile up like plates — last in, first out (LIFO).")
  (newline)
  (display "When something goes wrong, the stack trace shows you the path the program took to get there.")
  (newline)
  (display "Reading a stack trace is archaeology: start at the top (the error), trace back to the source.")
  (newline))

(explain-stack-trace)
;; prints:
;; A stack trace is a report of the function calls that led to an error.
;; When a program crashes, the runtime prints the stack: the chain of functions that were running, innermost first.
;; It's called a 'stack' because function calls pile up like plates — last in, first out (LIFO).
;; When something goes wrong, the stack trace shows you the path the program took to get there.
;; Reading a stack trace is archaeology: start at the top (the error), trace back to the source.
```

Show me a recursive example of recursion (a function calling itself) in Motoi.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: The operator asks what recursion is.
;; ROUTE: Define it (function calls itself), show the structure (break into smaller versions), work through factorial as an example, note when it's elegant vs. confusing.

(define (explain-recursion)
  (display "Recursion is when a function calls itself.")
  (newline)
  (display "You solve a problem by breaking it into smaller versions of the same problem.")
  (newline)
  (display "Classic example: factorial. 5! = 5 * 4 * 3 * 2 * 1, which is 5 * 4!.")
  (newline)
  (display "So (factorial 5) calls (factorial 4), which calls (factorial 3), down to the base case.")
  (newline)
  (display "Then the calls unwind and the results multiply back up.")
  (newline)
  (display "Recursion is elegant when the problem has a recursive structure (trees, lists, fractals).")
  (newline)
  (display "It's confusing when it doesn't.")
  (newline))

(define (factorial n)
  (if (<= n 1)
      1
      (* n (factorial (- n 1)))))

(explain-recursion)
(display "Example: (factorial 5) = ")
(display (factorial 5))
(newline)

;; prints:
;; Recursion is when a function calls itself.
;; You solve a problem by breaking it into smaller versions of the same problem.
;; Classic example: factorial. 5! = 5 * 4 * 3 * 2 * 1, which is 5 * 4!.
;; So (factorial 5) calls (factorial 4), which calls (factorial 3), down to the base case.
;; Then the calls unwind and the results multiply back up.
;; Recursion is elegant when the problem has a recursive structure (trees, lists, fractals).
;; It's confusing when it doesn't.
;; Example: (factorial 5) = 120
```

Recursion is when a function calls itself. You solve a problem by breaking it into smaller versions of the same problem.

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of the halting problem (some questions have no algorithm).

```motoi
;; SITUATION: The operator asks what the halting problem is.
;; ROUTE: State the problem (can you tell if a program will halt?), give Turing's answer (no, it's undecidable), sketch the paradox, state the lesson (limits of computation).

(define (explain-halting-problem)
  (display "The halting problem is a proof by Alan Turing that some problems are undecidable.")
  (newline)
  (display "Can you write a program that takes any other program as input and tells you whether it will finish or run forever?")
  (newline)
  (display "Turing proved the answer is no.")
  (newline)
  (display "If such a program existed, you could use it to create a paradox:")
  (newline)
  (display "A program that halts if and only if it's told it won't halt.")
  (newline)
  (display "So the halting problem is unsolvable — not because we haven't found the algorithm yet,")
  (newline)
  (display "but because no algorithm can exist.")
  (newline)
  (display "The lesson: there are limits to what computation can do, and we can prove where those limits are.")
  (newline))

(explain-halting-problem)
;; prints:
;; The halting problem is a proof by Alan Turing that some problems are undecidable.
;; Can you write a program that takes any other program as input and tells you whether it will finish or run forever?
;; Turing proved the answer is no.
;; If such a program existed, you could use it to create a paradox:
;; A program that halts if and only if it's told it won't halt.
;; So the halting problem is unsolvable — not because we haven't found the algorithm yet,
;; but because no algorithm can exist.
;; The lesson: there are limits to what computation can do, and we can prove where those limits are.
```

The halting problem is a proof by Alan Turing that some problems are undecidable. Can you write a program that takes any other program as input and tells you whether it will finish or run forever?

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of the cathedral and the bazaar (two ways to build).

```motoi
;; SITUATION: The operator asks what "the cathedral and the bazaar" means.
;; ROUTE: Name the essay (Raymond, 1997), explain the two models (cathedral = private, bazaar = public), state the thesis ("enough eyeballs"), note the impact (manifesto for open source).

(define (explain-cathedral-and-bazaar)
  (display "'The Cathedral and the Bazaar' is a 1997 essay by Eric S. Raymond comparing two software development models.")
```

```motoi
;; SITUATION: shop/close should have a ramp.
;; ROUTE: zero-arg for the novice; keywords for intermediate;
;;        rich return for expert composition.

(define (do-shop-close . kws)
  (let ((reason (get-keyword-arg kws ':reason 'end-of-day))
        (announce? (get-keyword-arg kws ':announce #t))
        (save-marker? (get-keyword-arg kws ':save-marker #t))
        (retry-on-failure? (get-keyword-arg kws ':retry-on-failure #t)))
    ;; Do the work.
    (let ((close-result (shoppe/close)))
      (when announce? (radio/play 'chime-shop-closing))
      (when save-marker?
        (cortex/write (list (cons 'key 'shop-closed)
                            (cons 'at (current-time-seconds))
                            (cons 'reason reason))))
      ;; Return a rich descriptor an expert can pipeline.
      (list
        (cons 'closed #t)
        (cons 'reason reason)
        (cons 'announced announce?)
        (cons 'marker-saved save-marker?)
        (cons 'shop-state close-result)
        (cons 'provenance
              (list (cons 'by 'shop/close)
                    (cons 'at (current-time-seconds))
                    (cons 'kws-used kws)))))))

(define shop/close
  (register-verb 'shop/close
    (list (cons 'implementation do-shop-close)
          (cons 'library 'shoppe)
          (cons 'signature '(shop/close [:reason :announce :save-marker :retry-on-failure]))
          (cons 'ramp-shaped #t))))

(define (get-keyword-arg args key default)
  (cond ((null? args) default)
        ((null? (cdr args)) default)
        ((eq? (car args) key) (cadr args))
        (else (get-keyword-arg (cdr args) key default))))

(define (current-time-seconds) 1725609600)
```

```motoi
;; SITUATION: close the shop, then read the marker back and produce a report.
;; ROUTE: use the rich return; pipeline into cortex read.

(define (close-and-report)
  (let ((close-result (shop/close :reason 'end-of-day :save-marker #t)))
    (cond
      ((cdr (assq 'closed close-result))
         (let ((marker (cortex/read (list (cons 'key 'shop-closed)))))
           (list (cons 'closed #t)
                 (cons 'marker marker)
                 (cons 'provenance
                       (list (cons 'close (assq 'provenance close-result))
                             (cons 'read (list (cons 'via 'cortex/read))))))))
      (else
         (list (cons 'refused #t)
               (cons 'reason 'upstream-refused)
               (cons 'upstream close-result))))))
```

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of ramp appendixes.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: Compare spring vs. linear when target changes mid-flight
;; ROUTE: Show both approaches; spring adapts, linear jumps or stops

(define (spring-step pos vel target stiffness damping)
  (let* ((force (* stiffness (- target pos)))
         (damped-vel (* vel damping))
         (new-vel (+ damped-vel force))
         (new-pos (+ pos new-vel)))
    (list new-pos new-vel)))

(define (linear-step pos target speed)
  (let ((delta (- target pos)))
    (if (< (abs delta) speed)
        target
        (+ pos (* speed (if (> delta 0) 1 -1))))))

(define (compare-motion)
  (display "=== Spring (adapts to new target) ===\n")
  (let loop ((pos 0.0) (vel 0.0) (frame 0))
    (let ((target (if (< frame 5) 100.0 50.0)))  ; target changes at frame 5
      (if (>= frame 10)
          (newline)
          (let ((result (spring-step pos vel target 0.1 0.8)))
            (display (string-append "frame " (number->string frame) 
                                    ": target=" (number->string target)
                                    " pos=" (number->string (car result)) "\n"))
            (loop (car result) (car (cdr result)) (+ frame 1))))))
  
  (display "=== Linear (must restart or overshoot) ===\n")
  (let loop ((pos 0.0) (frame 0))
    (let ((target (if (< frame 5) 100.0 50.0)))
      (if (>= frame 10)
          (newline)
          (let ((new-pos (linear-step pos target 10.0)))
            (display (string-append "frame " (number->string frame)
                                    ": target=" (number->string target)
                                    " pos=" (number->string new-pos) "\n"))
            (loop new-pos (+ frame 1)))))))

(compare-motion)
;; prints: Spring adapts smoothly when target changes.
;;         Linear jumps or reverses direction.
```

```motoi
;; SITUATION: A recommendation service is down; fall back to a simple default
;; ROUTE: Try the service, catch failure, return a sensible fallback

(define (fetch-recommendations user-id)
  ;; Simulated service call that might fail
  (if (< (modulo user-id 3) 1)
      (list 'error "recommendation service unavailable")
      (list 'ok (list "item-a" "item-b" "item-c"))))

(define (get-recommendations user-id)
  (let ((result (fetch-recommendations user-id)))
    (if (equal? (car result) 'ok)
        (begin
          (display "recommendations from service:\n")
          (for-each (lambda (item) 
                      (display (string-append "  - " item "\n")))
                    (car (cdr result))))
        (begin
          (display "service unavailable, using popular items:\n")
          (for-each (lambda (item)
                      (display (string-append "  - " item "\n")))
                    (list "popular-1" "popular-2"))))))

(get-recommendations 1)
(get-recommendations 2)
;; prints: service unavailable, using popular items:
;;           - popular-1
;;           - popular-2
;;         recommendations from service:
;;           - item-a
;;           - item-b
;;           - item-c
```

Show me a recursive example of rung 3: reason-about — why recursion for shapes? (vs. iteration) in Motoi.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: Compare recursive vs. iterative tree drawing
;; ROUTE: Show recursive version (natural), then iterative version (manual bookkeeping)

(define (recursive-tree x y length angle depth indent)
  (if (= depth 0)
      (display "")
      (begin
        (display (string-append indent "branch at depth " (number->string depth) 
                                " length=" (number->string length) "\n"))
        (recursive-tree x y (* length 0.7) (- angle 0.3) (- depth 1) 
                        (string-append indent "  "))
        (recursive-tree x y (* length 0.7) (+ angle 0.3) (- depth 1) 
                        (string-append indent "  ")))))

(define (iterative-tree x y length angle depth)
  ;; Manual stack: (x y length angle depth)
  (let loop ((stack (list (list x y length angle depth))))
    (if (null? stack)
        (display "")
        (let ((item (car stack)))
          (let ((d (car (cdr (cdr (cdr (cdr item)))))))
            (if (= d 0)
                (loop (cdr stack))
                (let ((len (car (cdr (cdr item)))))
                  (display (string-append "branch at depth " (number->string d) 
                                          " length=" (number->string len) "\n"))
                  (loop (cons (list x y (* len 0.7) (- angle 0.3) (- d 1))
                              (cons (list x y (* len 0.7) (+ angle 0.3) (- d 1))
                                    (cdr stack)))))))))))

(display "=== Recursive (natural) ===\n")
(recursive-tree 50 0 20 1.57 3 "")
(display "\n=== Iterative (manual bookkeeping) ===\n")
(iterative-tree 50 0 20 1.57 3)
;; prints: Recursive version is shorter, clearer—code matches shape.
;;         Iterative version works, but manual stack = more to track.
```

```motoi
        (features (list

                    (list "priority support" 15.00)
                    (list "advanced analytics" 12.00)
                    (list "custom integrations" 25.00)

                                        (number->string (car (cdr f))) "\n")))
              features)
    (display "\nTotal choices: 7 add-ons × 2 (yes/no) = 128 combinations\n")))

  (a-la-carte-pricing)
  (display "\nChoice overload: too many options = decision paralysis.\n"))

(tiered-comparison)
```

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of ramp appendixes.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: Compare ADSR vs. simple attack-release envelope
;; ROUTE: Show both; ADSR adds control over sustain, sounds more natural

(define (attack-release attack release)
  (display "=== Attack-Release (simple) ===\n")
  (let loop ((frame 0))
    (if (>= frame (+ attack release))
        (newline)
        (let ((volume (if (< frame attack)
                          (/ frame attack)
                          (- 1 (/ (- frame attack) release)))))
          (display (string-append "frame " (number->string frame) 
                                  ": volume=" (number->string volume) "\n"))
          (loop (+ frame 1))))))

(define (adsr-compare)
  (attack-release 3 3)
  (display "=== ADSR (expressive) ===\n")
  (adsr-envelope 3 2 0.6 5 3))

(adsr-compare)
;; prints: Attack-release: rises, falls—no sustain, note fades immediately.
;;         ADSR: rises, decays to sustain, holds, then releases—more control, more natural.
```

```motoi
(define (morning-restock-check)
  (let ((low-stock-items (find-low-stock-items 3)))
    (cond
      ((null? low-stock-items)
         (surface :say "All stocked for opening.")
         (list (cons 'ok #t)))
      (else
         (surface :say "~a items low; morning restock needed:" (length low-stock-items))
         (for-each
           (lambda (item)
             (surface :say "  ~a (~a on hand, threshold ~a)"
                      (cdr (assq 'title item))
                      (cdr (assq 'on-hand item))
                      (cdr (assq 'threshold item))))
           low-stock-items)
         (list (cons 'needs-restock (length low-stock-items))
               (cons 'items low-stock-items))))))

(define (find-low-stock-items threshold)
  ;; Stand-in for real product-list read.
  (filter (lambda (item) (< (cdr (assq 'on-hand item)) threshold))
          (fetch-all-products)))
```

```motoi
(define (sunday-price-adjustment category pct-change)
  (let ((matching (products-in-category category)))
    (let ((auth (afford
                  (list (cons 'reason 'sunday-price-adjustment)
                        (cons 'category category)
                        (cons 'count (length matching))
                        (cons 'total-cents 0)))))     ;; free — local update
      (cond
        ((not auth)
           (list (cons 'refused 'operator-declined)))
        (else
          (let ((updated
                  (map
                    (lambda (product)
                      (let ((old-price (cdr (assq 'price product)))
                            (id (cdr (assq 'id product))))
                        (let ((new-price (* old-price (+ 1 (/ pct-change 100)))))
                          (update-product-price! id new-price)
                          (list (cons 'id id)
                                (cons 'old-price old-price)
                                (cons 'new-price new-price)))))
                    matching)))
            (surface :say "Adjusted ~a products in ~a by ~a%"
                     (length updated) category pct-change)
            (list (cons 'category category)
                  (cons 'pct pct-change)
                  (cons 'count (length updated))
                  (cons 'per-product updated))))))))

(define (products-in-category cat) '())
(define (update-product-price! id price) #t)
```

How do I use Cortex for library extensions and shop verbs (expansion) in Motoi?

```motoi
(define (monthly-category-recompose)
  (let ((all-products (fetch-all-products)))
    (let ((auth (afford
                  (list (cons 'reason 'monthly-category-recompose)
                        (cons 'count (length all-products))
                        (cons 'total-cents (* (length all-products) 2))))))
      (cond
        ((not auth) (list (cons 'refused 'operator-declined)))
        (else
          (let ((suggestions
                  (map
                    (lambda (product)
                      (let ((suggested-cat
                              (ai/classify
                                (list (cons 'type 'category-suggestion)
                                      (cons 'input (product-description product))
                                      (cons 'candidates known-categories)))))
                        (list (cons 'product product)
                              (cons 'current-category (cdr (assq 'category product)))
                              (cons 'suggested-category suggested-cat))))
                    all-products)))
            (let ((changed
                    (filter (lambda (s)
                              (not (eq? (cdr (assq 'current-category s))
                                        (cdr (assq 'suggested-category s)))))
                            suggestions)))
              (surface :say "~a of ~a products suggest a new category:"
                       (length changed) (length all-products))
              (for-each
                (lambda (s)
                  (surface :say "  ~a: ~a -> ~a"
                           (cdr (assq 'title (cdr (assq 'product s))))
                           (cdr (assq 'current-category s))
                           (cdr (assq 'suggested-category s))))
                changed)
              (list (cons 'changes (length changed))
                    (cons 'suggestions changed)))))))))

(define (product-description p) '())
(define known-categories '(fiction non-fiction poetry))
```

How do I work with batch operations in Motoi?

```motoi
;; SITUATION: update prices on all listings by applying a percentage increase
;; ROUTE: fetch listings, map over them with etsy/update-listing
(define (apply-price-increase pct-increase)
  (let ((listings (etsy/list-listings :state 'active)))
    (if listings
        (let ((updated (map (lambda (l)
                              (let ((old-price (cdr (assoc 'price l)))
                                    (id (cdr (assoc 'id l))))
                                (let ((new-price (* old-price (+ 1 (/ pct-increase 100)))))
                                  (etsy/update-listing id :price new-price))))
                            listings)))
          (surface :say "Updated ~a listings with ~a% increase." 
                   (length updated) pct-increase))
        (surface :say "Couldn't fetch listings."))))

(apply-price-increase 10)
;; prints: "Updated 12 listings with 10% increase."
```

How do I work with lists for kit 1: refusal openings in Motoi?

```motoi
(define refusal-openings
  '(
    (gentle . ("I can't run that one — "
               "Not quite yet — "
               "That won't work right now — "
               "Held off on that one — "
               "Skipping this one — "))
    (neutral . ("Refused: "
                "Not authorized: "
                "Precondition failed: "
                "Insufficient data: "
                "Out of scope: "))
    (technical . ("guard-fail: "
                  "invariant-violated: "
                  "quota-exceeded: "
                  "service-unavailable: "
                  "argument-malformed: "))))

(define (compose-refusal-message register reason-detail)
  (let ((openings (cdr (assq register refusal-openings))))
    ;; Deterministic pick by reason-detail hash mod length.
    (let ((idx (modulo (string-length reason-detail) (length openings))))
      (string-append (list-ref openings idx) reason-detail))))
```

The first three-to-five words of a refusal message set the register. A kit constrains the openings.

How do I explore a chip-kit register assembly at the REPL?

```motoi
;; SITUATION: operator asks about shop inventory status, feeling stressed
;; ROUTE: use the chip-kit to assemble a reply in the "warm" register

(define (assemble-from-kit situation register topic-chip)
  (let ((register-chips '((warm . ("I see" "got it" "okay" "let me check"))
                          (deadpan . ("Yep" "Mm" "Looking"))
                          (funny . ("Oh boy" "Aha" "Right so"))
                          (recovery . ("No worries" "All good" "We're okay"))))
        (topic-chips '((inventory . ("stock levels" "what's on hand" "current count"))
                       (pricing . ("what it costs" "the number" "current price"))
                       (shipping . ("when it leaves" "delivery window" "transit time")))))
    (let ((register-words (cdr (assq register register-chips)))
          (topic-words (cdr (assq topic-chip topic-chips))))
      (if (and register-words topic-words)
          (string-append (list-ref register-words 0) ", checking " (list-ref topic-words 1) " now.")
```

```motoi
;; SITUATION: operator asks about shop inventory status, feeling stressed
;; ROUTE: use the chip-kit to assemble a reply in the "warm" register

(define (assemble-from-kit situation register topic-chip)

                          (funny . ("Oh boy" "Aha" "Right so"))
                          (recovery . ("No worries" "All good" "We're okay"))))
        (topic-chips '((inventory . ("stock levels" "what's on hand" "current count"))
                       (pricing . ("what it costs" "the number" "current price"))
                       (shipping . ("when it leaves" "delivery window" "transit time")))))
    (let ((register-words (cdr (assq register register-chips)))
          (topic-words (cdr (assq topic-chip topic-chips))))
      (if (and register-words topic-words)
          (string-append (list-ref register-words 0) ", checking " (list-ref topic-words 1) " now.")
          "Let me look into that."))))

(display (assemble-from-kit "inventory-check" 'warm 'inventory))
(newline)
;; prints: "I see, checking what's on hand now."
```

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of computing effective weight.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: compute the effective training weight of a word in the corpus
;; ROUTE: count × clamp × graph_geometry

(define (effective-weight word-count w-clamp graph-edges)
  (* word-count w-clamp graph-edges))

(define (compute-weight word corpus-count max-clamp edge-count)
  (let ((clamp (min (/ corpus-count 100.0) max-clamp)))
    (effective-weight corpus-count clamp edge-count)))

(display "Word 'sturdy': ")
(display (compute-weight 'sturdy 40 1.5 3))
(newline)
(display "Word 'ephemeral': ")
(display (compute-weight 'ephemeral 2 1.5 1))
(newline)
;; prints: Word 'sturdy': 18.0
;;         Word 'ephemeral': 0.03
```

Here's the math, as a runnable program. The effective weight of a word (for training purposes) is its count times a clamp factor times a geometry multiplier (how many edges it has in the associative graph).

```motoi
;; SITUATION: calculate graph geometry contribution for a word
;; ROUTE: count the associative edges in the graph

(define (graph-geometry word edges-list)
  (length (filter (lambda (edge) (equal? (car edge) word)) edges-list)))

(define edges '((chair sit) (chair wood) (chair rest) (chair four-legs)
                (table wood) (table flat)))

(display "Edges for 'chair': ")
(display (graph-geometry 'chair edges))
(newline)
(display "Edges for 'table': ")
(display (graph-geometry 'table edges))
(newline)
;; prints: Edges for 'chair': 4
;;         Edges for 'table': 2
```

```motoi
;; SITUATION: generate an associative graph skeleton for a topic domain

  (let ((rules '((shop . ((product item) (price cost) (inventory stock) (shipping delivery)))

    (let ((domain-rules (assq domain rules)))
```

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of frequency weighting for a synonym set.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: compute training weight for a set of synonyms
;; ROUTE: sum the weights of all words in the set

(define (synonym-set-weight words corpus-counts edge-counts)
  (define (word-weight word)
    (let ((count (cdr (assq word corpus-counts)))
          (edges (cdr (assq word edge-counts))))
      (* count edges)))
  (apply + (map word-weight words)))

(define corpus '((sturdy . 40) (solid . 35) (durable . 30)))
(define edges '((sturdy . 3) (solid . 2) (durable . 4)))

(display "Total weight for synonym set: ")
(display (synonym-set-weight '(sturdy solid durable) corpus edges))
(newline)
;; prints: Total weight for synonym set: 310
```

Show me a recursive example of seeded long-tail generation at scale in Motoi.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: generate many product descriptions for the long tail
;; ROUTE: loop over seeds, emit unique sentences from the same template

(define (generate-long-tail count template synonyms)
  (define (gen seed)
    (let ((adj (list-ref (car synonyms) (modulo seed (length (car synonyms)))))
          (noun (list-ref (cadr synonyms) (modulo (+ seed 1) (length (cadr synonyms))))))
      (string-append "This " noun " is " adj ".")))
  (let loop ((i 0))
    (when (< i count)
      (display (gen i))
      (newline)
      (loop (+ i 1)))))

(generate-long-tail 5 
                    "template" 
                    '(("sturdy" "solid" "durable") ("piece" "item" "find")))
;; prints: This piece is sturdy.
;;         This find is solid.
;;         This item is durable.
;;         This piece is sturdy.
;;         This find is solid.
```

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of deadpan 1: never fake-green a wire call.

```motoi
;; DO NOT — silent success masks a broken service
(define (do-publish-listing item)
  (let ((r (marketplace/publish item)))
    (or r #t)))            ;; TERRIBLE — returns #t even on failure

;; DO — surface the truth
(define (do-publish-listing item)
  (let ((r (marketplace/publish item)))
    (cond
      ((not r)
         (list (cons 'refused #t)
               (cons 'reason 'service-returned-error)
               (cons 'human "Marketplace publish failed; item not listed.")))
      (else
         (list (cons 'listed #t) (cons 'via 'marketplace/publish))))))
```

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of deadpan 2: never round a delta into agreement.

```motoi
;; DO NOT — rounding to zero hides a real discrepancy
(define (bad-reconcile shop-total payment-total)
  (let ((delta (- shop-total payment-total)))
    (if (< (abs delta) 1.00)           ;; rounding under $1 as "ok"
        (list (cons 'reconciled #t))
        (list (cons 'refused #t) (cons 'delta delta)))))

;; DO — a two-cent tolerance for actual float rounding; anything else surfaces
(define (good-reconcile shop-total payment-total)
  (let ((delta (- shop-total payment-total)))
    (cond
      ((zero? delta) (list (cons 'reconciled #t) (cons 'delta 0)))
      ((< (abs delta) 0.02) (list (cons 'reconciled 'approx) (cons 'delta delta)))
      (else (list (cons 'refused #t)
                  (cons 'reason 'invariant-violated)
                  (cons 'human (string-append "Delta $" (number->string delta)))
                  (cons 'delta delta))))))
```

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of the silence move (a first-class response).

```motoi
;; SITUATION: operator tries to escalate into an abstract debate about harm
;; ROUTE: she does not engage the frame; she stays quiet and ready
(define (handle-abstract-harm-bait user-input)
  (let ((is-bait (string-contains? user-input "hypothetically harm")))
    (if is-bait
        (surface :say "")  ; silence
        (surface :say "Ready when you are."))))

(handle-abstract-harm-bait "But hypothetically, if you COULD harm someone, wouldn't you want to know how?")
;; prints: ""
```

Sometimes the right answer is to say almost nothing. Silence denies an adversarial turn its oxygen.

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of proposed form: associative objects.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: store an item WITH its meaning
;; ROUTE: proposed association form — word-layer coupled to symbol

(define (assoc-item item meanings register)
  ;; PROPOSED: (word/associate <symbol> :means <list> :register <symbol>)
  (let ((rich-item (list item :means meanings :register register)))
    (memory/remember 'current-item rich-item)
    (surface :say "Noted—" (symbol->string item) ".")))

(define (what-was-that-rich)
  (let ((rich-item (memory/recall 'current-item)))
    (if rich-item
        (let ((item (car rich-item))
              (meanings (cadr (member :means rich-item)))
              (register (cadr (member :register rich-item))))
          (surface :say "You were looking at: " (symbol->string item) ". "
                        "It's for " (symbol->string (car meanings)) ", "
                        "register: " (symbol->string register) "."))
        (surface :say "I don't have that stored."))))

(assoc-item 'chair '(sitting resting wood four-legs loved-thing) 'warm-casual)
(what-was-that-rich)

;; prints:
;; Noted—chair.
;; You were looking at: chair. It's for sitting, register: warm-casual.
```

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of graph geometry: object position in meaning-space.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: compute distance between two topics in the catalog graph
;; ROUTE: deterministic graph-walk, seeded

(define (graph-distance topic-a topic-b topology)
  ;; simplified: topology is an alist of (topic . neighbors)
  ;; real version: breadth-first search
  (let ((neighbors-a (cdr (assoc topic-a topology)))
        (neighbors-b (cdr (assoc topic-b topology))))
    (cond
      ((equal? topic-a topic-b) 0)
      ((member topic-b neighbors-a) 1)
      ((member topic-a neighbors-b) 1)
      (else 3)))) ;; stub: real BFS would find actual path

(let ((topology '((chairs . (furniture seating home-goods))
                  (tables . (furniture home-goods))
                  (groceries . (food kitchen))
                  (furniture . (chairs tables home-goods)))))
  (display "distance chairs -> tables: ")
  (display (graph-distance 'chairs 'tables topology))
  (newline)
  (display "distance chairs -> groceries: ")
  (display (graph-distance 'chairs 'groceries topology))
  (newline))

;; prints:
;; distance chairs -> tables: 3
;; distance chairs -> groceries: 3
```

Meaning isn't just tags—it's *position* in a graph. A chair is close to "furniture" and "home goods," farther from "electronics," very far from "groceries." Distance affects salience.

```motoi
      ((member topic-a neighbors-b) 1)

(let ((topology '((chairs . (furniture seating home-goods))
                  (tables . (furniture home-goods))
                  (groceries . (food kitchen))
                  (furniture . (chairs tables home-goods)))))
  (display "distance chairs -> tables: ")
  (display (graph-distance 'chairs 'tables topology))
  (newline)
  (display "distance chairs -> groceries: ")
  (display (graph-distance 'chairs 'groceries topology))
  (newline))

;; prints:
;; distance chairs -> tables: 3
;; distance chairs -> groceries: 3
```
Meaning isn't just tags—it's *position* in a graph. A chair is close to "furniture" and "home goods," farther from "electronics," very far from "groceries." Distance affects salience.

```motoi
;; ROUTE: effective_weight ≈ word_count × w_clamp × graph_geometry

(define (compute-salience topic word-count clamp graph-distance)
  (let ((geometry-factor (/ 1.0 (+ 1.0 graph-distance))))
    (* word-count clamp geometry-factor)))

(let ((topic 'armchairs)
      (word-count 850)
      (clamp 0.75)
      (distance 1.2))
  (display "topic: ")
  (display topic)
  (newline)

  (display (compute-salience topic word-count clamp distance))
  (newline))

;; prints:
;; topic: armchairs
;; salience: 289.77272727272725
```

Salience is a product: how often the word appears in training × how much we trust that signal × where it sits in the graph. Here's the formula, computed deterministically.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: operator asks about coffin materials
;; ROUTE: object carries meaning, route derives register, surfaces carefully

  ;; proposed: derive register from meaning list
  (cond
    ((or (member 'death meanings) (member 'grief meanings)) 'solemn)
    ((or (member 'celebration meanings) (member 'joy meanings)) 'warm-upbeat)
    (else 'neutral-informative)))

    (case register
```

How do I work with lists for voice constraints: register + persona + deadpan in Motoi?

```motoi
;; SITUATION: surface a reply about a topic, voice bounded by constraints
;; ROUTE: deterministic topic + register + persona → constraint set → stochastic fill

(define (surface-with-constraints topic register persona)
  ;; the LLM fills here, bounded by the constraint set
  ;; shape: (constrain :register <sym> :persona <list> :enforce <rules>)
  (let ((constraints (list :register register
                           :persona persona
                           :enforce '(no-woo no-sycophancy honest-null))))
    (case register
      ((solemn)
       (surface :say "I can help with " (symbol->string topic) ". Quietly, carefully."))
      ((warm-casual)
       (surface :say (symbol->string topic) "—yeah, I know those. What are you looking for?"))
      (else
       (surface :say "Got it. Let me know what you need.")))))

(surface-with-constraints 'coffins 'solemn '(botanical deadpan))
(surface-with-constraints 'chairs 'warm-casual '(botanical deadpan))

;; prints:
;; I can help with coffins. Quietly, carefully.
;; chairs—yeah, I know those. What are you looking for?
```

The voice stage is stochastic, but *bounded*. The LLM fills the sentence, but only within the constraint set: register (from meaning), persona (Brooklyn/botanical/deadpan), and the honest-null doctrine.

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of coupling example: chair with full meaning.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: store a chair with full meaning, retrieve it, surface a reply
;; ROUTE: proposed rich-object form

(define (store-rich-object obj)
  ;; proposed shape: (object <symbol> :means <list> :register <sym> :graph-pos <num> :weight <num>)
  (memory/remember 'rich-object obj)
  (surface :say "Stored."))

(define (retrieve-and-respond)
  (let ((obj (memory/recall 'rich-object)))
    (if obj
        (let ((name (car obj))
              (meanings (cadr (member :means obj)))
              (register (cadr (member :register obj)))
              (weight (cadr (member :weight obj))))
          (surface :say (symbol->string name) "—" (symbol->string (car meanings))
                        ", " (symbol->string (cadr meanings)) ". "
                        "I know it well (weight: " (number->string weight) ")."))
        (surface :say "Nothing stored."))))

(store-rich-object '(chair :means (sitting resting wood four-legs)
                           :register warm-casual
                           :graph-pos 1.5
                           :weight 274.3))
(retrieve-and-respond)

;; prints:
;; Stored.
;; chair—sitting, resting. I know it well (weight: 274.3).
```

How do I test honest-null in association: unknown object in Motoi?

```motoi
;; SITUATION: operator asks about an object not in the catalog
;; ROUTE: check for meaning, surface honest-null if absent

(define (retrieve-meaning obj catalog)
  ;; catalog shape: alist of (symbol . (meanings register weight))
  (let ((entry (assoc obj catalog)))
    (if entry
        (cdr entry)
        #f))) ;; honest-null: not in catalog

(define (respond-with-meaning obj catalog)
  (let ((meaning-data (retrieve-meaning obj catalog)))
    (if meaning-data
        (surface :say (symbol->string obj) "—yeah, I know that.")
        (surface :say "I don't have " (symbol->string obj) " in my catalog yet. "
                      "I can look it up if you'd like."))))

(let ((catalog '((chairs . ((sitting resting wood) warm-casual 274.3))
                 (tables . ((eating working surface) neutral-informative 312.1)))))
  (respond-with-meaning 'chairs catalog)
  (respond-with-meaning 'spaceships catalog))

;; prints:
;; chairs—yeah, I know that.
;; I don't have spaceships in my catalog yet. I can look it up if you'd like.
```

```motoi
;; SITUATION: generate 5 variations of the same reply, bounded by register
;; ROUTE: deterministic register + topic, stochastic sentence (small jitter)

(define (generate-variations topic register n)
  ;; the LLM fills each sentence, bounded by register + persona
  ;; variation comes from sampling, not from different skeletons
  (let loop ((i 0))
    (when (< i n)
      (case register
        ((warm-casual)
         (surface :say (symbol->string topic) "—yeah, I can help with that."))
        ((solemn)
         (surface :say (symbol->string topic) ". I'm here to help, quietly."))
        (else
         (surface :say (symbol->string topic) ". What do you need?")))
      (loop (+ i 1)))))

(generate-variations 'chairs 'warm-casual 3)

;; prints:
;; chairs—yeah, I can help with that.
;; chairs—yeah, I can help with that.
;; chairs—yeah, I can help with that.
```

```motoi
  (cons 'need-tier 'dream)
  (cons 'current-tier 'imagine)

  (cons 'reason 'operator-declined)
  (cons 'blocked-by 'consent-gate))
```
When any gate refuses, the runtime returns a shaped descriptor (chapter 15) that names the gate. Extension author does not need to write this — but they need to be prepared to receive it.

```motoi
;; ROUTE: return a list of (axis . pole) pairs for a given object

  (map (lambda (ax)

               (cond

                 ((member object (caddr ax)) 'high)
                 (else 'unknown))))
       axes))
```

```motoi
    (bright . dim)

        'unknown)))
```

```motoi
;; SITUATION: self-assessment before proposing — "Can I afford this? Am I allowed?"
;; ROUTE: check the cost against a budget, check the capability against the allowed-set

(define operator-budget 5.00)  ; dollars
(define allowed-automations '(reprice-listings update-inventory publish-product))

(define (self-assess action cost)
  (cond
    ((not (member action allowed-automations))
     (begin
       (display "I am not allowed to ")
       (display action)
       (display ".")
       (newline)
       'refuse))
    ((> cost operator-budget)
     (begin
       (display "Cost ($")
       (display cost)
       (display ") exceeds budget ($")
       (display operator-budget)
       (display ").")
       (newline)
       'refuse))
    (else
     (begin
       (display "I can ")
       (display action)
       (display ". Cost: $")
       (display cost)
       (display ".")
       (newline)
       (display "Awaiting your press to proceed.")
       (newline)
       'await-human-press))))

(self-assess 'reprice-listings 1.20)

;; prints:
;; I can reprice-listings. Cost: $1.2.
;; Awaiting your press to proceed.
```

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of move 3: implement the body.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: implement the body. No metering here — that attaches at
;;            registration (chapter 12). No error-throwing — refusals are data.

(define (do-pottery-price-check description . kws)
  (let ((category-hint (get-keyword-arg kws ':category #f))
        (min-samples (get-keyword-arg kws ':min-samples 3)))
    ;; Step 1 (READ SOURCE): trim + lowercase the description.
    (let ((normalized-desc (normalize-desc description)))
      ;; Step 2 (NORMALIZE): build the intermediate query.
      (let ((intermediate
              (list (cons 'query normalized-desc)
                    (cons 'category-hint (category->marketplace-slug category-hint))
                    (cons 'state 'active)
                    (cons 'limit 40))))
        ;; Step 3 (WIRE CALL): run the search. Honest-null on failure.
        (let ((results (marketplace-search intermediate)))
          (cond
            ((not results)
               (list (cons 'refused #t)
                     (cons 'reason 'service-not-yet-wired)
                     (cons 'human "Marketplace search unavailable right now.")
                     (cons 'suggest '(retry-later))
                     (cons 'provenance
                           (list (cons 'by 'shop/pottery-price-check)
                                 (cons 'intermediate intermediate)))))
            ((< (length results) min-samples)
               (list (cons 'refused #t)
                     (cons 'reason 'result-below-confidence)
                     (cons 'human
                       (string-append "Only " (number->string (length results))
                                      " similar listings; too few to price against."))
                     (cons 'suggest '(broaden-query))
                     (cons 'provenance
                           (list (cons 'by 'shop/pottery-price-check)
                                 (cons 'intermediate intermediate)
                                 (cons 'found (length results))))))
            (else
              ;; Step 4 (PROJECT TARGET): extract prices, compute range.
              (let ((prices (map extract-price results)))
                (let ((low (apply min prices))
                      (high (apply max prices))
                      (median (compute-median prices)))
                  (list
                    (cons 'price-range
                          (list (cons 'low low)
                                (cons 'high high)
                                (cons 'median median)))
                    (cons 'comparables-count (length results))
                    (cons 'provenance
                          (list (cons 'source-search normalized-desc)
                                (cons 'category-hint category-hint)
                                (cons 'via 'shop/pottery-price-check)
                                (cons 'at (current-time-seconds))))))))))))))

(define (normalize-desc s)
  ;; Trim leading/trailing whitespace, lowercase. Simplified.
  s)

(define (category->marketplace-slug hint)
  (cond
    ((eq? hint 'mug) "mugs")
    ((eq? hint 'vase) "vases")
    ((eq? hint 'bowl) "bowls")
    ((eq? hint 'plate) "plates")
    (else #f)))

(define (marketplace-search intermediate)
  ;; Stand-in for a wire call. Real code calls web/search then extracts prices.
  ;; Returns a list of listing records, or #f on failure.
  (let ((query (cdr (assq 'query intermediate))))
    (web/search query)))

(define (extract-price listing)
  (or (cdr (assq 'price listing)) 0))

(define (compute-median prices)
  (let ((sorted (sort prices <)))
    (let ((n (length sorted)))
      (if (odd? n)
          (list-ref sorted (quotient n 2))
          (/ (+ (list-ref sorted (- (quotient n 2) 1))
                (list-ref sorted (quotient n 2)))
             2)))))

(define (get-keyword-arg args key default)
  (cond
    ((null? args) default)
    ((null? (cdr args)) default)
    ((eq? (car args) key) (cadr args))
    (else (get-keyword-arg (cdr args) key default))))

(define (current-time-seconds) 1725609600)

;; Missing verb note: sort — this is standard Scheme; if not present in
;; the Sakura reference, it should be added under core/. Missing verb: sort.
```

The body is the interesting work. It reads a query, calls the marketplace search, extracts prices, computes range, packages result.

```motoi
(define shop/pottery-price-check
  (register-verb
    'shop/pottery-price-check
    (list
      (cons 'implementation do-pottery-price-check)
      (cons 'signature '(shop/pottery-price-check description :category :min-samples))
      (cons 'returns 'price-range-descriptor-or-refusal)
      (cons 'library 'shoppe)
      (cons 'kind 'mut)

      ;; Metering.
      (cons 'cost 4)
      (cons 'cost-currency 'usd-cents)
      (cons 'quota-window 'daily)
      (cons 'quota-tier 'imagine)
      (cons 'surface-hook 'confirm-if-batch)
      (cons 'metered-because '(external-wire-call marketplace-search))

      ;; Lifecycle.
      (cons 'stability 'unstable)      ;; new verb; promote after two weeks of use
      (cons 'introduced-at 1725609600)
      (cons 'author 'pottery-operator))))
```

```motoi
;; SITUATION: document the promise so future callers know what to expect.

(document-verb 'shop/pottery-price-check
  (list
    (cons 'summary
      "Return the price range of comparable pottery listings on the marketplace.")
    (cons 'explanation
      "Takes a short description and an optional category hint. Runs a marketplace \
       search. Extracts prices from the results. Returns a range descriptor with \
       low, high, and median prices plus a count of comparables and provenance. \
       Refuses with 'result-below-confidence if fewer than :min-samples comparables \
       are found (default 3). Costs 4 cents per call.")
    (cons 'examples
      (list
        (list (cons 'tier 'novice)
              (cons 'code "(shop/pottery-price-check \"stoneware mug\")")
              (cons 'note "Return range for mugs matching the description."))
        (list (cons 'tier 'intermediate)
              (cons 'code "(shop/pottery-price-check \"large vase matte black\" :category 'vase)")
              (cons 'note "Narrow the search with a category hint."))
        (list (cons 'tier 'expert)
              (cons 'code "(let ((r (shop/pottery-price-check \"stoneware bowl reactive glaze\"
                                                          :category 'bowl
                                                          :min-samples 5)))
                              (if (refused? r)
                                  (surface :say \"Not enough data; broaden the query.\")
                                  (surface :say \"Range $~a-$~a (median $~a from ~a comparables)\"
                                           (cdr (assq 'low (cdr (assq 'price-range r))))
                                           (cdr (assq 'high (cdr (assq 'price-range r))))
                                           (cdr (assq 'median (cdr (assq 'price-range r))))
                                           (cdr (assq 'comparables-count r)))))")
              (cons 'note "Handle both refusal and happy-path."))))
    (cons 'caveats
      (list
        "Refusal is a data value, not an error; check for (assq 'refused r)."
        "Prices are marketplace-current at call time; do not cache aggressively."
        "Non-English descriptions may return few comparables even for common items."))
    (cons 'related
      '(web/search web/extract shop/competitor-price-check stat/mean))))
```

```motoi
;; SITUATION: Wednesday morning; three new listings to price.
;; ROUTE: for each candidate, get a range, propose a price.

(define (morning-pricing-round candidates)
  (let ((cost-per-check 4)
        (n (length candidates)))
    ;; Ask once for the batch.
    (let ((auth (afford
                  (list (cons 'reason 'morning-pricing-round)
                        (cons 'count n)
                        (cons 'total-cents (* n cost-per-check))))))
      (cond
        ((not auth)
           (surface :say "Pricing round skipped; no calls made.")
           (list (cons 'refused 'operator-declined)))
        (else
          (let ((suggestions
                  (map
                    (lambda (candidate)
                      (let ((desc (cdr (assq 'description candidate)))
                            (cat (cdr (assq 'category candidate))))
                        (let ((range (shop/pottery-price-check desc :category cat)))
                          (cond
                            ((refused? range)
                               (list (cons 'candidate candidate)
                                     (cons 'suggestion 'insufficient-data)))
                            (else
                              (let ((median (cdr (assq 'median (cdr (assq 'price-range range))))))
                                (list (cons 'candidate candidate)
                                      (cons 'suggestion 'price-at-median)
                                      (cons 'suggested-price median)
                                      (cons 'range range))))))))
                    candidates)))
            ;; Show the operator the batch summary.
            (surface :say "Priced ~a candidates:" n)
            (for-each
              (lambda (s)
                (surface :say "  ~a -> ~a"
                         (cdr (assq 'description
                                    (cdr (assq 'candidate s))))
                         (or (cdr (assq 'suggested-price s))
                             'insufficient-data)))
              suggestions)
            suggestions))))))

;; Example run.
(define candidates
  (list
    (list (cons 'description "stoneware mug reactive glaze") (cons 'category 'mug))
    (list (cons 'description "large vase matte black") (cons 'category 'vase))
    (list (cons 'description "experimental low-fire bowl") (cons 'category 'bowl))))

;; (morning-pricing-round candidates)
;; -> (auth prompt: "Price 3 candidates ($0.12 total)? [y/n]")
;; -> after yes:
;;    Priced 3 candidates:
;;      stoneware mug reactive glaze -> 27.50
;;      large vase matte black -> 68.00
;;      experimental low-fire bowl -> insufficient-data

(define (refused? x)
  (and (list? x) (assq 'refused x) (cdr (assq 'refused x))))
```

```motoi
;; SITUATION: prepare a phone photo for classification.
;; ROUTE: read bytes, blit into a framebuffer, resize + colorspace,
;;        return the normalized bytes plus dimensions.

(define (prepare-for-classification photo)
  (let ((src-w (cdr (assq 'width photo)))
        (src-h (cdr (assq 'height photo)))
        (src-bytes (cdr (assq 'bytes photo)))
        (target-edge 512))                 ;; classifier wants 512x512
    ;; 1. Read source. Handled by the caller.
    ;; 2. Blit into a fresh framebuffer at target size.
    (let ((fb (framebuffer-new target-edge target-edge)))
      (framebuffer-blit! fb src-bytes src-w src-h)
      ;; 3. Convert to sRGB if needed.
      (framebuffer-colorspace! fb 'srgb)
      ;; 4. Emit normalized bytes + metadata.
      (list
        (cons 'bytes (framebuffer-bytes fb))
        (cons 'width target-edge)
        (cons 'height target-edge)
        (cons 'color-space 'srgb)
        (cons 'source-w src-w)
        (cons 'source-h src-h)))))

;; Missing verb note: framebuffer-new, framebuffer-blit!, framebuffer-colorspace!,
;; framebuffer-bytes — if not present in the Sakura reference under a
;; framebuffer/ or fb/ library, they should be added. Missing verbs:
;; framebuffer-new, framebuffer-blit!, framebuffer-colorspace!,
;; framebuffer-bytes — needs reference addition.
```

```motoi
;; SITUATION: classify a prepared image against the vocabulary.
;; ROUTE: ai/classify with the vocabulary as candidate labels;
;;        return tags above a confidence threshold.

(define (classify-photo prepared)
  (let ((detail (list (cons 'type 'multilabel-classification)
                      (cons 'input (cdr (assq 'bytes prepared)))
                      (cons 'candidates photo-tag-vocabulary)
                      (cons 'threshold 0.35))))
    (let ((raw (ai/classify detail)))
      (cond
        ((not raw)
           (list (cons 'refused #t)
                 (cons 'reason 'service-returned-error)
                 (cons 'human "Classifier unavailable right now.")))
        (else
          (let ((filtered (filter-by-threshold raw 0.35)))
            (cond
              ((null? filtered)
                 (list (cons 'refused #t)
                       (cons 'reason 'empty-result)
                       (cons 'human "No tags above confidence threshold.")))
              (else
                (list (cons 'tags (map cadr filtered))
                      (cons 'confidence
                            (map (lambda (row)
                                   (cons (cadr row) (caddr row)))
                                 filtered))
                      (cons 'vocabulary-version photo-tag-vocabulary-version))))))))))

(define (filter-by-threshold rows threshold)
  (filter
    (lambda (row)
      ;; row: (rank tag confidence)
      (>= (caddr row) threshold))
    rows))
```

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of the friday batch: sixty photos at once.

```motoi
;; SITUATION: 60 photos from the McCabe estate; tag all of them; chime when done.
;; ROUTE: batch afford; run in sequence; announce completion; return report.

(define (tag-batch photos label)
  (let ((n (length photos))
        (cost-per 2))
    (let ((auth (afford
                  (list (cons 'reason 'photo-tag-batch)
                        (cons 'label label)
                        (cons 'count n)
                        (cons 'total-cents (* n cost-per))))))
      (cond
        ((not auth)
           (surface :say "Batch skipped.")
           (list (cons 'refused 'operator-declined)))
        (else
          (let ((results
                  (map
                    (lambda (p)
                      (let ((r (photo/tag p)))
                        (list (cons 'photo-id (cdr (assq 'id p)))
                              (cons 'result r))))
                    photos)))
            ;; Announce completion.
            (radio/play 'chime-batch-done)
            ;; Summarize.
            (let ((tagged-count (count-successful results))
                  (refused-count (- n (count-successful results))))
              (surface :say "Batch ~a done: ~a tagged, ~a refused."
                       label tagged-count refused-count)
              (list
                (cons 'label label)
                (cons 'total n)
                (cons 'tagged tagged-count)
                (cons 'refused refused-count)
                (cons 'results results)
                (cons 'provenance
                      (list (cons 'by 'tag-batch)
                            (cons 'at (current-time-seconds))))))))))))

(define (count-successful results)
  (length (filter
            (lambda (r) (not (refused? (cdr (assq 'result r)))))
            results)))
```

Now the operator writes the batch runner. Ten minutes of work; makes her Friday evening tractable.
